1:
The chapter opens after the terror of Huldah's prophecy, and the witnesses are struck less by the summons itself than by the resolve behind it. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the scene: Josiah had received word from God that there was no preventing the ruin of Jerusalem, that he should deliver only his own soul; yet he did not sit down in despair and do nothing for his country because he could not do everything he wished. He would do his duty and leave the event to God. A public reformation was resolved on, and so the king summoned a general assembly of the states, the elders and representatives of Judah and Jerusalem, to meet him in the house of the Lord with the priests and prophets, that all joining in it, the act might become national and the more likely to avert national judgments. Henry adds the political aside that parliaments are no diminution to the honour of good princes but a great support to them.

The reformed John Gill keeps to the plain fact: Josiah sent messengers throughout the land and convened all the principal men at Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in who was gathered and why. Beside the priests, Josiah assembled the prophets, perhaps Jeremiah and Zephaniah among them, that the solemn renewal of the covenant might be carried through with their co-operation, and that they might then undertake, by their impressive preaching in Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, to make the people conscious of the earnestness of the covenant duties they had just assumed. Keil notes that where Kings names the prophets, the parallel in 2 Chronicles 34:29 names the Levites, probably because Levites are mentioned with priests in other cases of the kind; and that the verb "he read" means he had it read, since reading the law in the temple devolved on the priests as its keepers, per Deuteronomy 31:9.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, attends to the text of the opening clause. "They gathered," he says, is probably better read "there gathered," following the Syriac and Vulgate, while Chronicles, the Septuagint and the Arabic have "he gathered"; and "all the elders" are simply the representatives of the nation. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, surveying the whole program the chapter will unfold, frames the assembly as the first act of a sweeping reform in which Josiah reads the book of the covenant, binds king and people to it, and then sets out to purge the land. The witnesses do not divide; together they present a doomed king refusing to be paralysed by a sentence he cannot revoke, gathering his whole people to hear God's word and to bind themselves afresh to it.
2:
"And the king went up into the house of the LORD" — and the witnesses fasten on the striking figure of a king who reads the law with his own voice. The reformed John Gill notes only the movement, that Josiah went up to the temple from his palace; but the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke finds the whole scene singular: the high priest, the scribes, the priests and prophets, and all the elders were gathered, and yet it was the king who read the book of the covenant lately found. It is strange, Clarke says, that neither Hilkiah nor Jeremiah nor Zephaniah, certainly present, did the reading; and he concludes that Josiah considered himself a kind of mediator between God and the people, and therefore read and made the covenant himself.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees the king read it himself, and turns it into a lesson: Josiah did not think it beneath him to be a reader, any more than Solomon thought it beneath him to be a preacher, or David to be a doorkeeper in the house of God; all people are concerned to know the Scripture, and all in authority to spread the knowledge of it. As to the prophets present, Wesley names Jeremiah, Zephaniah, and Urijah, or else the sons of the prophets.

The grammatical-historical witnesses weigh the wording. Charles Ellicott identifies "the prophets" as the numerous members of the prophetic order, then a distinct class repeatedly named in Jeremiah; the Targum's reading "the scribes" he rejects as anachronistic, since that class hardly existed so early, while Chronicles and some manuscripts read "the Levites." The claim that all the men of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem were present he calls a natural hyperbole, for the temple court would not hold the whole population, and "small and great" he glosses as high and low. On who read, Ellicott is more cautious than Clarke: it may have been the king, but not necessarily, for he who does a thing by another does it by himself, and the priests were charged to read the law to the people every seventh year, per Deuteronomy 31:9. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the substance: the king stood by the pillar, as in 2 Kings 11:14, and read in their ears all the words of the book of the covenant found in the house of the Lord. The witnesses converge on the astonishing propriety of it — a king who will not delegate the reading of God's word, but sets the example of reverence he means his people to follow.
3:
The covenant itself is now struck, and the witnesses gather around the pillar where the king stood and the pledge he took. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agree on the place: Josiah stood upon the pillar or, as Clarke renders it, upon the stairs or raised platform — the brazen scaffold that Solomon had made and on which the kings were accustomed to stand when they addressed the people, as at the dedication of the temple in 2 Chronicles 6:13. It was, Gill says, the king's appointed station.

On the substance of the covenant Clarke is fullest, laying it out in three articles: in general, to walk after Jehovah and have no gods besides him; then to take his law for the regulation of their conduct; and in particular, to bend the whole heart and soul to its observance, so that they might have not merely religion without but piety within. To all this the people stood up, Clarke notes, giving their consent and binding themselves to obedience. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott stresses the definite article — this was "the covenant," the covenant so often broken — to which Josiah pledged himself to walk after the Lord and imposed the same pledge on the people; and "stood to the covenant" means simply that the people entered it, taking the king's own pledge upon themselves.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads their standing as the sign of consent and concurrence, which they likely expressed by rising as the king himself stood when he took the oath. And he draws the practical moral the book has taught throughout: it is of good use, with all possible solemnity, to oblige ourselves to our duty, for he that bears an honest heart does not startle at such assurances. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the legal force of the language: the king bound himself solemnly to walk after the Lord, following him and keeping his commandments, and all the people entered into the covenant — not merely, as some would render it, stood firm or continued in a covenant already made, but actually came into it there and then. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that this was a real and binding transaction, king and nation together vowing, upon the newly recovered book, to be the Lord's; and Henry's earlier maxim hangs over the scene — fast bind, fast find.
4:
With the covenant made, the great purge begins, and here the witnesses pour out their fullest comment. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry stands back to marvel at the sheer depth of the dunghill Josiah had to carry away: after eighteen years of a godly reign, when the king came to make inquisition for idolatry, the abominations found in Judah and Jerusalem — in Salem, in Zion, where God's dwelling-place was — were almost incredible. Even in the house of the Lord, the temple Solomon built, there were vessels made for the worship of Baal, of the grove or Ashtaroth, and of all the host of heaven; though Josiah had suppressed the worship, the utensils had been carefully preserved, to be used again whenever the restraint should be lifted. Henry sees in the whole reformation the law of Deuteronomy 7:5, that the monuments of idolatry must be destroyed, much more those of Israel's idolatry, in whom it was more impious and perfidious than in the Canaanites.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott attend to the officers and the objects. Keil notes that the eradication had already begun before the covenant, according to 2 Chronicles 34:3, and was now carried to completion; "the priests of the second order" are the common priests as distinguished from the high priest, and "the keepers of the door" the Levites who kept the threshold. Ellicott, following Thenius, would read the singular — the priest of the second rank, the high priest's deputy — unless the heads of the twenty-four courses, the chief priests of the New Testament, are meant; and the vessels were burned, according to the law of Deuteronomy 12:3, without Jerusalem as unclean, in the fields of Kidron north-east of the city. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and John Wesley add the reasons. Clarke observes that the vessels, though no longer used, would be an incentive to idolatry if left, and so must be destroyed; Wesley explains "the grove" as the image of the grove, since images are commonly called by the names of what they represent.

On one detail the witnesses feel a difficulty. The ashes were carried unto Bethel, and Wesley reads this as an act of contempt — Josiah would not leave even the ashes in his kingdom, but flung them upon the place that had been the chief seat and throne of idolatry, to pollute and disgrace it. Ellicott is more troubled: it is strange, he says, that Chronicles says nothing of it, and stranger that the ashes of the vessels should be sent to Bethel but not those of the idols or the fragments of the altars, so that the text appears to him corrupt. The reformed John Gill keeps to the priests, taking "the second order" of the two chief priests next to the high priest, of the lines of both Eleazar and Ithamar. The witnesses do not divide over the fact of the cleansing; they divide only over the puzzle of the ashes' journey, and unite in reading the whole as the thorough riddance the law demanded.
5:
"And he put down the idolatrous priests" — and the witnesses divide interestingly over just who these Chemarim were. The reformed John Gill takes them as priests so called because they wore black clothes, whereas the priests of the Lord were clothed in white linen. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke traces the name to a root meaning to be scorched or blackened, because their business was to attend the sacrificial fires; and he notes, with some tartness, that the Jews in derision call Christian ministers by this very name because of their black garments — "why we should imitate, in our sacerdotal dress, those priests of Baal, is strange to think and hard to tell."

The grammatical-historical witnesses press a finer distinction. Keil and Delitzsch insist that the Chemarim were not the idol-priests or prophets of Baal, but priests whom the kings of Judah had appointed to burn incense on the high places — men who did service sometimes for a Jehovah degraded into a Baal, and sometimes to actual idols. He points to the same word in Hosea 10:5, of the priests of the calf at Bethel, and in Zephaniah 1:4; and he distinguishes them from the plain idolaters who burnt incense to Baal, the sun, the moon, and all the host of heaven. Charles Ellicott agrees that the word here denotes the unlawful priests of Jehovah as contrasted with the priests of the Baal in the next clause, though he questions whether it really means "black-robed," since priests wore white throughout the ancient world; he offers instead a derivation from a root meaning to weave — a weaver of spells or charms, since magic was an invariable companion of false worship.

On the objects of that worship the witnesses converge with small variations. Clarke observes that though Baal was indeed the sun, the two are here distinguished, because Baal was worshipped under many forms; and "the planets," the Hebrew mazzaloth, he takes with the Vulgate as the twelve signs of the zodiac. Ellicott concurs that mazzaloth means the signs of the zodiac, a word used in the Targums for the stars as they were thought to govern human destiny, and he rebukes the critic who would tax the Hebrew historians with confusing Baal and the sun, when the Assyro-Babylonian system itself kept Bel and Samas distinct. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley, more briefly, takes the Chemarim as the highest rank of priests, employed in the highest work of burning incense. The witnesses agree on the substance: a whole apparatus of false priesthood, from irregular Jehovah-priests to open star-worshippers, was swept away at a stroke.
6:
The Asherah that had stood within the very house of the Lord is now dragged out to be destroyed, and the witnesses agree closely on both the object and the insult done to it. The reformed John Gill takes "the grove" not as a real grove of trees but as a carved image — the idol Ashtoreth or Astarte, which Theodoret and others identify with Venus — set up in the temple and now ordered out. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make the history precise: this was the image of Asherah that Manasseh had placed in the temple, removed after his return from Babylon according to 2 Chronicles 33:15, but replaced by Amon; Josiah had it burned and ground to powder in the Kidron valley, the verb pointing to the crushing of the metal covering of the image. He notes that the valley had already been made a place of defilement — Asa had burned an idol there in 1 Kings 15:13, and Hezekiah had carried the idolatrous filth thither — and that a burial-ground for the common people lay there.

On that last point the witnesses are of one mind, and they hear in it a studied contempt. The powder of the image was cast upon the graves of the children of the people, which the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and Keil alike take to mean the burial-place of the common people. Charles Ellicott sharpens the note of scorn: "the graves of the sons of the people" are the common graves, and to scatter the dust of the idol there was a mark of utter contempt; he observes that the parallel in 2 Chronicles 34:4 paraphrases it as "the graves of them that sacrificed unto them," so that the very worshippers were dishonoured in their dust along with their god. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "the people" in the same light — that idolatrous people, as the Chronicler explains.

The witnesses do not divide; they render together a single deliberate act of desecration, in which the goddess who had defiled the temple is reduced to ashes and mingled with the graves of the meanest of her devotees, that idolatry might be made not only forbidden but loathsome. What Henry elsewhere called the burying of idolatry out of sight, as a dead and forgotten thing, is here begun in the dust of the Asherah cast among the dead. The place that Manasseh's apostasy had polluted is cleansed by an act of pollution, and the sacred tree that stood in Jehovah's house is scattered where no worshipper could gather it again without contracting the uncleanness of the tomb.
7:
Hard by the temple stood houses of a still fouler kind, and the witnesses handle the verse with the mixture of candour and reserve the subject demands. The reformed John Gill explains that near the temple were apartments where the worshippers of idols prostituted their bodies to one another in unnatural sin, committed in honour of their idols, to which vile affections they had been judicially given up; the Hebrew calls such persons "holy ones" by a bitter irony, though Abarbanel would take them rather for the idolatrous priests reckoned holy, and the Targum reads simply "the houses of things consecrated to idols."

The grammatical-historical witnesses agree on the horror and locate it exactly. Keil and Delitzsch take the houses of the Kedeshim as tents or huts erected in the temple court for the paramours, in which women also wove tent-shrines for Asherah; Charles Ellicott renders them the cabins of the Kedeshim within the house of the Lord — males, perhaps eunuchs, who prostituted themselves like women in honour of the Asherah, as in 1 Kings 14:24 and Hosea 4:14. The passage shows, Ellicott says, that the last infamy of Canaanite nature-worship had been established in the very sanctuary of Jehovah — the revolt of Judah could go no farther. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees that the word covers all kinds of prostitutes and abusers of themselves with mankind, and that the women wove curtains or tent-coverings for the places where the rites of the impure goddess were performed.

On the weaving-women a small uncertainty remains. Ellicott confesses that what the women wove — the Hebrew word is simply "houses" — is not clear: perhaps portable tabernacles or shrines of the goddess for sale to the worshippers, or screens for their own foul rites. John Wesley canvasses the same options: curtains to draw before the idols, or garments for the service of the grove, or little chapels of woven work like the silver shrines of Acts 19:24, or tents made of those curtains. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley also states the theological root plainly: sodomy was a part of idol-worship, done to the honour of the idols and by the appointment of the impure spirits worshipped in them. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that corporal and spiritual whoredom had gone together in the temple itself, and that Josiah's levelling of these houses tore down the vilest growth the apostasy had produced.
8:
The reform now reaches out from the temple to the whole land, and the witnesses trace its geography and its awkward last clause. The reformed John Gill takes the priests brought out of the cities of Judah to be the sons of Aaron who had served in the high places there. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the strategy: Josiah summoned all the Levitical priests to Jerusalem so that they might no longer carry on their illegal worship in the country, and defiled the altars of the high places on which they had burned incense, from Geba to Beersheba — Geba, the present Jeba some three hours north of Jerusalem, being the northern frontier of Judah, and Beersheba the southern frontier of Canaan, so that the phrase means throughout the whole kingdom. Charles Ellicott adds that Beersheba was itself a specially frequented high place, named by Amos, and that the migration of these priests was meant to be rid of the illegitimate worship once for all.

The obscure "high places of the gates" the witnesses read together. Ellicott takes them as altars erected within the gates, that those entering or leaving the city might make an offering to ensure success in their business. As for "the gate of Joshua the governor of the city," he confesses Joshua an unknown personage and the reference uncertain — whether a city gate named after him or the gate of his residence — but reasonably supposes the governor's house lay near the principal gate, on the left as one entered, with several high places standing in the open space before it. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the gate-altars as raised to the honour of tutelary gods, whom after the heathen manner they owned as protectors of their city; and he draws the moral of the governor's name being recorded — that it shows Josiah's zeal and impartiality in rooting out all monuments of idolatry, without respect to the great persons concerned in them.

Adam Clarke, briefly, takes the gate of Joshua as the place where the governor heard causes, near which public altars had stood. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that no corner of the kingdom and no rank of person was spared — from the far north to the far south, and up to the very door of the city's chief magistrate, the illegal altars were thrown down and defiled. Wesley notes the manner of defilement, by burning dead men's bones upon them or putting them to some other unclean use, so that the places should be shunned as polluted for ever.
9:
A single word of mercy tempers the purge, and the witnesses dwell on the mildness shown to the country priests who had served at the high places. The reformed John Gill notes that though these priests were removed from the high places, they were not admitted to officiate at the altar of the Lord at Jerusalem, having offered in forbidden places. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make the distinction exact: Josiah did not allow them to perform the sacrificial service of the law upon the temple altar, but he did allow them to eat of the sacred altar-gifts intended for the priests, according to Leviticus 6:9 — only not at a holy place, but at home among their brethren, so that they were placed on a par with the priests disqualified for service by a bodily defect in Leviticus 21:17.

Charles Ellicott presses the same comparison: these priests were considered incapacitated for the altar by their former illegal ministrations, and might not even eat their share of the meat-offerings in company with the legitimate priests, but had to take their meals apart, among their brethren, that is, in their own company. He notes that to eat of the unleavened bread is a technical phrase meaning to live upon the offerings, and supposes these irregular priests were now employed in the inferior duties of the temple. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the arrangement in the same key: because they had sacrificed on high places, even to the true God, they were not thought fit to minister at the temple, yet as they belonged to the priesthood they had a right to their support, and so were treated as priests with some infirmity, per Leviticus 21:22.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out both the justice and the clemency of it. Their exclusion from the priestly function, he says, was a just punishment for the corruption of God's worship and the transgression of so plain and positive a law as Deuteronomy 12:11 — worse in them who had more knowledge to discern God's will; yet in allowing them the meal-offerings Josiah mitigated their punishment, shutting them out from spiritual services while granting them their necessary provisions. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the discipline was measured — the men who had ministered in forbidden places lost their office but not their bread, and the very reform that swept away their altars still fed them from the table of the Lord.
10:
Josiah's zeal now descends into the valley of Hinnom to defile the very hearth of child-sacrifice, and the witnesses gather around the dreadful name Topheth. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley both derive it from toph, a drum or timbrel, beaten at the burning of the children so that their shrieks might not be heard by their parents, lest the parents repent and snatch them away; Gill compares the drums and trumpets sounded in India at the burning of widows, for the same purpose of drowning the cries. The place Josiah defiled, Gill says, by casting filth and unclean things into it, in contempt of the idolatry practised there and to turn men's minds from it.

The grammatical-historical witnesses prefer a harsher etymology. Keil and Delitzsch derive Topheth from a root meaning to spit or spit out, so that the name marks the place as an object of abhorrence; and they note that from Gehinnom, the valley of Hinnom, the Rabbins formed the word Gehenna, used in Matthew 5:22 for hell and hell-fire, with special reference to the children burnt there to Moloch. Charles Ellicott records both derivations — the burning place if from a Persian root meaning to burn, or the abomination if the Hebrew has been modified to suggest spitting — and identifies the valley of the children of Hinnom, taking Moloch as another form of melech, king. The purpose of the defilement, the verse itself declares, was that no man might make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire to Molech.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lingers over the place and its later fame. He cites Jerome that Topheth was once a fine and pleasant spot, well watered and adorned with gardens; here the rites of Moloch were performed, and to this valley all the filth of the city was afterwards carried, with perpetual fires kept up to consume it, so that it became a type of hell and is so used in the New Testament. But Clarke will not swallow every legend: the lurid description of Topheth as a public human shambles, where the limbs of executed criminals were quartered by thousands, he sets down with a sceptical query — "on what authority do such descriptions rest?" The witnesses do not divide over the essential act; they agree that Josiah made the shrine of the cruellest of the idolatries a place of pollution and horror, so that the valley whose name became a byword for hell was first, by his hand, made a byword for shame.
11:
Among the strangest relics of the apostasy were living horses stabled at the temple gate in honour of the sun, and the witnesses debate their nature and use. The reformed John Gill insists they were not images but real living horses, consecrated to the sun and very probably given by Manasseh and Amon; he marshals the classical evidence that horses were sacred to the sun among the Massagetae, the Persians, the Babylonians and the Ethiopians, and reports two opinions — that of the Jewish commentators, that the horses were kept for the sun-worshippers to ride out at dawn to meet the rising sun, and that of Herodotus, Xenophon, Strabo and others, that such nations slew the horses as burnt-offerings to the sun, offering the swiftest of creatures to the swiftest of their gods.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reason from the text that these were living horses and not statues, since the horses were only cleared away while the chariots were burned; horses were held sacred to the sun by many nations, and Israel had received the practice, along with sun-worship itself, from Upper Asia, perhaps through the Assyrians. The kings who gave them he identifies as Ahaz, Manasseh and Amon. Charles Ellicott agrees the horses drew the chariots of the sun in solemn processions, and were also sacrificed, the whole growing out of the poetic conception of the sun's course as a fiery chariot and steeds; and he fixes their place — kept in the outer court near the entrance, toward the cell of Nathan-melech the chamberlain, in the porticoes or cloisters, the Persian word parwarim.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses hold the two purposes together. Adam Clarke reports Jarchi's opinion that the sun's adorers mounted these horses each morning to meet him at his rising, recalls the four horses the poets gave the sun's chariot, and leaves it open whether the animals were living or sculptured, though he thinks the latter likelier. John Wesley takes them as consecrated to the sun to signify the swiftness of his motion, kept either to be sacrificed or to draw the chariots in which the worshippers rode out at dawn, as Xenophon reports of the Armenians and Persians. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, in his survey of the reformation, had already caught the pity of it — that horses and chariots meant to be the chariots and horsemen of Israel should be squandered on the sun, and that when Josiah burned the chariots of the sun they never resembled that flame so much as when they blazed. The witnesses divide only over whether the horses were slain or ridden or harnessed; they agree that a costly and absurd superstition, planted at the Lord's own threshold, was torn out by the roots.
12:
The purge turns next to the rooftop altars, and the witnesses explain both their situation and the haste with which Josiah tore them down. The reformed John Gill notes that the flat roofs of Judean houses could bear altars, and that these on the upper chamber of Ahaz stood on the roof of the royal palace, built there — very probably by Manasseh and Amon — for daily incense to the sun, the height perhaps chosen as nearer the heavens. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree the altars were dedicated to the host of heaven, as Zephaniah 1:5 and Jeremiah 19:13 attest, and originally built by Ahaz; since Hezekiah would surely have removed them, they must have been restored by Manasseh and Amon, so that "the kings of Judah" means those three. The upper chamber's exact location he cannot fix, but supposes it an upper room of one of the buildings in the temple court, perhaps at a gate Ahaz had built.

Charles Ellicott concurs that a roof-chamber in one of the temple courts, perhaps built over a gateway, is meant, and that the altars were for the star-worship especially practised on housetops, citing Jeremiah 32:29. He finds a vivid touch in the verb: the Hebrew, he says, probably means the king ran from thence, marking the haste with which the work was done — a stroke the Targum softens to "removed" and the Septuagint to "pulled down," but which he would keep, unless with Kimchi and Thenius one reads that Josiah caused to run, that is, hurried the altars away. The dust of them was cast, he adds, over the wall of the temple enclosure into the ravine beneath.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the rooftop altars as a mark of how mad the people were upon their idols — not content with all their public high places, they set up more upon their house-tops for the worship of the heavenly bodies — and takes the casting of the dust into Kidron as Josiah's way of showing his detestation and abolishing the very remembrance of them. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that even the private, domestic idolatry of kings, perched on the palace roof and long tolerated, was now beaten down and its dust flung into the polluted brook.
13:
Now the king confronts the oldest scandal of all — the high places Solomon himself had raised on the Mount of Olives — and the witnesses explain the place, the name, and the guilt. The reformed John Gill takes "the high places that were before Jerusalem" to include both those within the city and at its gates and those without. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch locate them on the southern height of the Mount of Olives, built by Solomon for Ashtoreth, Chemosh and Milcom, as 1 Kings 11:7 records; these Josiah defiled, reducing the monuments to ruins, cutting down the Asherim, and filling their places with human bones, which by Numbers 19:16 polluted the ground. The mountain they call, with the tradition of the Church, the mount of destruction or offence.

On that ominous name the witnesses offer a shared philology. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, following Jarchi and the Targum, says the Mount of Olives was properly the mount of unction, hammishchah, but that Scripture changed the word to hammashchith, of corruption, because of the idolatry practised there. Charles Ellicott agrees that the southern summit was so called for its idolatry, and that it still bears the name Hill of Offence from the Vulgate's mons offensionis; he adds that the word rendered "corruption" may originally have meant "anointing," from the olive oil produced there, so that the name was once simply the equivalent of the German Oelberg, before it was modified to express detestation of idol-worship. "Before Jerusalem," he explains, means to the east of it, since one faced the sunrise to fix the quarters, so that the right hand was the south.

Ellicott alone hesitates over what exactly Josiah destroyed. Since the verse does not say the high places were pulled down but only defiled, he thinks they may have been merely sacred sites — a levelled surface of rock with holes scooped for libations, such as have been found in Palestine — for it is hardly conceivable that chapels built by Solomon would have survived to Josiah's day, when even Hezekiah had done away with the high places dedicated to Jehovah. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley meets the difficulty differently: these were not Solomon's own altars, which some earlier king had removed, but altars built by Manasseh or Amon in the same place and after his example, and so branded with Solomon's name by the Holy Ghost as a lasting rebuke to him and a warning to others. The witnesses do not divide over the deed; they agree that the most venerable and the most royally sanctioned idolatry in all Judah was, at last, defiled and dishonoured.
14:
The verse completes the work of the last, and the witnesses read it as the seal set upon Josiah's defilement of the high places. The reformed John Gill takes the images broken in pieces to be those of Ashtoreth, Chemosh and Milcom, new images set up by Manasseh or Amon when they rebuilt the high places. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the pair more exactly as "the pillars and the asherahs" — the standing stones and sacred trees — and notes that the Hebrew construction does not tie them strictly to the high places just named, so that the reference is probably general. On the filling of their places with the bones of men, Ellicott observes that the word is a technical term for the station or site of an idol, the same maqom that still designates holy sites in Palestine.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the point of the act in a sentence: to fill these places with the bones of men was to expose them to the utmost defilement to which anything could be subjected. John Wesley enlarges it into the grim symmetry of the whole reformation. The bones, he says, were those of the idolatrous priests, taken out of their graves as v.18 will show; and as Josiah carried the ashes of the images to the graves to mingle them with dead men's bones, so he carried dead men's bones to the places where the images had stood, that by both ways idolatry might be rendered loathsome. Dead men and dead gods, Wesley concludes with Henry, were indeed much alike, and fittest to go together.

The witnesses do not divide, and the plain verse gathers up the logic of everything Josiah has done. Every monument of the false worship is not merely removed but polluted; the sacred trees are cut down, the images shattered to powder, and the sites where they stood are sown with the uncleanness of the grave, so that no one might ever again resort to them without contracting defilement. Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as a closer definition of the defiling named in the verse before, added as a simple continuation. What the witnesses hear in it, above the archaeology, is the thoroughness of a reformer who would leave the idols no bones to gather and no ground to return to — a burial of idolatry so complete that even its ruins were made to reek of death.
15:
Josiah's zeal now crosses the old border into the northern kingdom, and the witnesses fasten on the altar at Bethel — the very shrine of Jeroboam's calf-worship. The reformed John Gill notes simply that this was the altar and high place Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin, had made for the worship of the calf there. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that Josiah would not rest with cleansing his own kingdom but destroyed the temples, altars and idols of the former ten tribes as well; grammatically, they insist, "the high place" stands in apposition to "the altar," describing the altar at Bethel as an illegal sanctuary. He burned its buildings, ground to powder everything of stone or metal, both altar and idol, and burned the Asherah found there.

A quiet but telling agreement runs between the grammatical-historical witnesses and the prophet Hosea. Keil observes that the golden calf once set up at Bethel seems, by Hosea 10:5, to have been carried off by the Assyrians and supplanted, after the heathen colonists came, by idols of Baal and Astarte. Charles Ellicott makes the same point: no mention is made here of what had been the chief object of worship at Bethel, the golden bullock, because it had already been carried away to Assyria as the prophet foretold — an indirect but striking confirmation of Hosea 10:6. Ellicott also puzzles over the burning of a stone structure: perhaps the high place resembled one of the dolmens found by the hundred in Palestine, and fire was kindled to crack the huge slabs so that the fragments might the more easily be crushed. On the grammar he agrees the connecting "and" is wanting in the Hebrew, supplied rightly for the sense by the versions.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley accounts for Josiah's reach into Bethel by history: the city was probably now under Judah, added long before by Abijah, and since the ten tribes were carried away many cities had put themselves under Judah's protection; the golden calf, he agrees, was gone, but Josiah would leave no remains of that idolatry. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole episode, marks how the king who came from Judah now executed what the man of God from Judah had foretold — and how, the country once cleared of its old leaven, they turned to keep the passover. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the fountain-head of the northern apostasy, standing three centuries, was at last broken down, burned, and stamped to dust by a king of David's line.
16:
As Josiah turned, his eye fell on the tombs in the hillside, and the witnesses see in the moment both the king's personal zeal and the reach of an old prophecy. The reformed John Gill notes that Josiah not only ordered the destruction but saw it done himself, turning from the altar at Bethel to spy the sepulchres. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the act and its point: to desecrate the idolatrous site for all time, the king had human bones taken from the graves upon the mountain and burned on the altar, thereby fulfilling the word spoken in Jeroboam's reign by the prophet who came out of Judah, in 1 Kings 13:2; yet he spared the tomb of that prophet himself, as 1 Kings 13:26 and following relate.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott both notice that the Hebrew text seems to have suffered here. Clarke observes that the verse is much fuller in the Septuagint and the Hexaplar Syriac, which add that the prophecy was proclaimed when Jeroboam stood by the altar at the feast, and that, turning about, he cast his eyes on the sepulchre of the man of God. Ellicott agrees that some words have fallen out: a transcriber's eye, he suggests, wandered from one "man of God" to the other, so that the Septuagint's fuller reading is to be preferred, and Josiah returned just as he was on the point of going away, catching sight of the tombs on the hill opposite — not the hill where the high place stood. Both, with Keil, refer the reader back to the original prophecy in 1 Kings 13:2.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the wonder of the fulfilment and the character of the king. Josiah's care and zeal were so great, he says, that he would not trust his officers with these things but would see them done with his own eyes; and the words the men of the city recalled had been spoken three hundred years before it was done. The witnesses do not divide. They marvel together that a prophecy uttered against this very altar, naming Josiah by name three centuries earlier, should now be accomplished to the letter by a king who happened to turn and see a row of graves — the burning of the idolaters' bones upon their own altar being, as the narrative frames it, the exact vindication of the word of the Lord.
17:
"What title is that that I see?" — the king's question, and the townsmen's answer, draw the witnesses to the monument that saved a prophet's bones. The reformed John Gill takes the "title" for a high and conspicuous monument with an inscription, more remarkable than the graves around it, which caught Josiah's eye; he reports a Jewish tradition, recorded by Kimchi, that nettles and thistles grew on one side of the grave and fragrant herbs on the other, though he sets no great store by it, and prefers the more sober point — that the old prophet, believing the man of God's words would be fulfilled, had both directed that his own body be laid in the same grave and had a distinguished pillar erected over it, which people in later times took care to keep up, so that by this means the bones of the man of God, and of the old prophet buried with him, were alike preserved from the fire.

The grammatical-historical witnesses agree on the sense of the rare word. Charles Ellicott renders the king's question "What is yonder monument, or memorial stone?", comparing the "sign" of Ezekiel 39:15 and the "waymarks" of Jeremiah 31:21, and refers back to the burial recorded in 1 Kings 13:29. Adam Clarke agrees that there was a stone, an image, or an inscription here, and that the old prophet had plainly taken care to make the place sufficiently remarkable. Keil and Delitzsch identify the ziyyun as a sepulchral monument, probably a stone set up upon the grave.

The witnesses do not divide, and the little scene carries a quiet providence they all feel. The men of the city can still tell Josiah whose tomb it is — the sepulchre of the man of God which came from Judah, and proclaimed these things that the king had now done against the altar of Bethel — so that across three hundred years the messenger and his message are remembered together, and the very monument the deceiving old prophet raised out of remorse now serves, in Josiah's hand, to mark off the one grave in all that hillside that must not be touched. What began as an old man's superstition or penitence becomes the means by which a faithful prophet's rest is honoured at the hour his oracle is fulfilled.
18:
"Let him alone; let no man move his bones." The verse is brief, and the witnesses with it, but they catch the tenderness and the exactness of the king's order. The reformed John Gill reads the command plainly: the bones of the man of God were not to be taken out of the grave as the others had been, but left undisturbed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens both verbs. The king's word, he says, is better rendered "Let him rest"; and the narrator's report that "they let his bones alone" uses a different verb, meaning that they suffered his bones to escape — that is, escape the disturbance visited on all the tombs about them. So the one grave was passed over untouched in the midst of a general exhumation.

On the companion buried there the witnesses agree. "With the bones of the prophet that came out of Samaria," the verse adds, and Ellicott explains that this simply designates the old prophet who had deceived the Judean man of God as a citizen of the northern kingdom, called Samaria after its capital; the two skeletons lay together, and for the sake of the faithful prophet the deceiver's bones were spared as well, as 1 Kings 13:31 had arranged. Adam Clarke, briefly, refers the reader to that same passage, where the old prophet's request to be buried beside the man of God is recorded.

The witnesses do not divide, and the sparse notice yields a lesson they leave mostly implicit. The old prophet's dying scheme — to lay his bones next to the man of God, so that when the prophecy against Bethel came true his own dust might be spared — is here quietly vindicated. His cunning, or his faith, has its reward: because he shares the grave of a true prophet, he shares the true prophet's immunity. The bones of the obedient and the disobedient rest together, and together they are let alone, while the bones of the idolatrous priests round about are dragged out and burned upon the altar they served. The single tomb the king protects becomes a small monument to the difference God makes between his servant and his servant's enemies, even in the dust.
19:
The reformation now sweeps through the wider country of the old northern kingdom, and the witnesses weigh both the deed and the king's warrant for it. The reformed John Gill takes "all the houses of the high places" to mean the temples of the idols there and the dwellings of their priests. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report that Josiah destroyed all the high-place houses in the cities of Samaria just as he had done at Bethel, offering up their priests upon their own altars and burning men's bones upon them to defile them; and Charles Ellicott confirms that these were the temples or chapels attached to the high places, adding from 2 Chronicles 34:6 that the king's zeal carried him as far north as Naphtali.

The harder question — by what right a king of Judah acted so freely beyond his own borders — the witnesses answer in different keys. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke grounds it in sacred right: Israel now had no king, and Josiah, of the blood royal of Judah, had a direct claim to the whole land, and at this time an especial commission from God to reform every abuse throughout all the ground once given to the twelve sons of Jacob; therefore he had every right to carry his work into the Samaritan territory. Ellicott offers instead a political explanation, or two. Perhaps, as a vassal of Assyria, Josiah enjoyed a certain authority over the old domains of the ten tribes — his later opposition to Necho favouring the idea that he recognised the Assyrian as his suzerain; or perhaps the remnant of Israel was drawn toward Judah and its king as the surviving heirs of their race's past glory, and sympathised in the reformation, as 2 Chronicles 34:9 may suggest. A third possibility he allows is that, with the fall of Assyria now imminent, no notice was taken of Josiah's proceedings in the west at all.

So the witnesses divide, mildly, over the ground of Josiah's authority — a God-given commission to the whole inheritance of Jacob, or a vassal's licence, or the simple sympathy of a leaderless people, or the distraction of a dying empire. They do not divide over the fact. From Bethel to Naphtali the shrines that the kings of Israel had built to provoke the Lord to anger were torn down and defiled by a son of David, extending the reformation of Judah over the graves of the northern kingdom.
20:
The purge of the north reaches its grimmest point, and the witnesses face the slaughter of the high-place priests upon their own altars. The reformed John Gill distinguishes carefully: those slain were the idolatrous priests who had sacrificed to Baal and other heathen deities, while the priests who had merely burned incense in high places to the true God were spared, though not allowed to officiate at the altar of the Lord. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the same contrast with the mild treatment shown the Judean priests earlier in the chapter: the word here is stronger, "he slaughtered," for the Judean priests were Levites, but these were heathenish priests, dealt with under the law of Deuteronomy 17:2. Adam Clarke agrees in a sentence that the lives of these men, as corrupters of the people, were forfeited to the law.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley both read the act as the fulfilment of an ancient word. Keil notes that Josiah offered up the priests upon the altars on which they had sacrificed, and burned men's bones upon them to defile them, before returning to Jerusalem. Wesley sees in the whole scene the completion of the famous prophecy of 1 Kings 13:2; and he fills in the history that made it possible — that after the king of Assyria withdrew, many of the Israelites who had escaped the conqueror returned with their priests to their own land and to their old trade of idol-worship, ascribing perhaps their deliverance to the very idols, so that Josiah found a living idolatry to punish. Ellicott adds the note of the sceptic Thenius, who grants the event historical precisely because the prophecy is undoubtedly modelled upon it.

The witnesses do not divide over the justice of the deed, harsh as it is, and they agree on its meaning. The men who had officiated at these altars are made sacrifices upon them; the bones of the dead are heaped on the stones to pollute them; and a sentence spoken three centuries before, against this very apostasy, is carried out to the letter. Where in Judah the erring Jehovah-priests kept their bread and lost only their office, in Samaria the priests of a false god lost their lives — the difference measuring, for these witnesses, the difference between an irregular worship of the true God and the open service of idols.
21:
The reformation of tearing-down now gives way to the reformation of building-up, and the witnesses mark the turn to positive worship in the command to keep the passover. The reformed John Gill notes that the king commanded not the people of Jerusalem only but the whole kingdom to keep the feast. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the order "Hold a passover," and observes that Josiah had the precedent of Hezekiah, who had likewise signalised his religious revolution with a solemn passover in 2 Chronicles 30:1; the phrase "in the book of this covenant" he corrects to "in this book of the covenant" — the book Hilkiah had found in the temple, which had given the impulse to the whole reforming movement, and which now supplies the pattern for the feast.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that our account gives the passover very briefly — merely recording the king's command, and leaving the execution to be supplied — while the fuller narrative stands in 2 Chronicles 35. What most distinguished it, they say, was that all the nation took part, not Judah and Benjamin only but the remnant of the ten tribes, and that it was kept in strict accordance with the precepts of the Mosaic book of the law.

The witnesses do not divide, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the sequence, had already drawn out its wisdom: when Jehu destroyed the worship of Baal he took no heed to walk in the ordinances of God, but Josiah considered that we must learn to do well and not only cease to do evil, and that the way to keep out every abominable custom is to keep up all the instituted ordinances, as Leviticus 18:30 teaches. So the passover was not an afterthought but the crown of the cleansing: having purged the land of the old leaven, the king gathered his people to the feast of their redemption, a memorial of the deliverance out of Egypt and a token of their renewed communion with the God who had brought them out. The tearing-down was for the sake of this building-up; the reformation reached its goal not in an empty land but in a keeping of the feast.
22:
The historian pauses to measure this passover against all the passovers of the past, and the witnesses weigh in what its uniqueness consisted. The reformed John Gill takes "the days of the judges" with the parallel in Chronicles to mean the days of Samuel, the last of the judges, so that no such passover had been held since his time. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch specify what set it above every other: that all the nation, Judah and the remnant of Israel together, took part in it, and that it was kept in strict conformity to the Mosaic law. Charles Ellicott renders the clause "For there was not holden a passover like this passover," and treats this verse and the next as a parenthesis in which the historian underlines the phrase "as it is written in this book of the covenant" — no passover from the time of the Judges onward had been celebrated in such strict accordance with the Law.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press that the distinction lay in quality, not numbers. Adam Clarke insists there had not been one held on purer principles, more heartily joined in by the people, more literally consecrated, or more religiously observed; the words do not speak of the multitude present but of the manner and the spirit, the particulars of which stand in 2 Chronicles 35. John Wesley enlarges the same thought: it was celebrated with such solemn care, such preparation, so many sacrifices, and such universal joy of all good men — a joy the greater for their memory of the wicked and miserable times under Manasseh and Amon, and for the good hopes they now had of their nation's settlement and of the averting of God's threatened judgments. None of the kings, Wesley adds, had taken such care to prepare priests and people, to observe every rite, and to purge out all uncleanness.

A textual note divides the grammatical-historical witnesses only slightly. Ellicott records that the Septuagint omits the particle of comparison, and that Thenius on that ground thinks the text of Kings may have been altered to harmonise with the Chronicler's account of Hezekiah's passover — an inference Ellicott judges weighty out of all proportion to its slight data, noting that the Chronicler himself repeats this very verse at the close of his narrative in 2 Chronicles 35:18. The witnesses do not divide over the substance. They agree that in fidelity to the law, in the union of the whole people, and in the depth of its joy, Josiah's passover stood without equal in the memory of the nation.
23:
The single date fixes the whole reformation in time, and the two witnesses at hand draw out its significance. The reformed John Gill reckons from it how swiftly Josiah must have moved: since the passover fell on the fourteenth of Nisan, and the king had accomplished all that this chapter and the last record before that day, the reformation must have been begun very early in the eighteenth year — the same year, Gill notes, in which the repairs of the temple were finished and the book of the law found. The purging of a whole kingdom, that is, was crowded into the opening months of a single year, and brought to its climax at the feast of the passover.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording and to the larger claim. He would omit the connecting word "wherein," and, following Ewald, takes the sense of these two verses to be that the passover had never been so celebrated before — especially in two respects: the offerings brought over and above the paschal lamb, as required in Deuteronomy 16:2, and the strict unity of the one place of the festival, according to Deuteronomy 16:5. The older critical assumption, that no passover at all had ever been held before Josiah's, he dismisses as by then obsolete even among advanced critics and unworthy of serious discussion.

The witnesses do not divide, and the bare notice of the year gathers up the meaning of the whole. This eighteenth year of Josiah — the year of the temple's repair, of the finding of the law, of the covenant renewed and the idols destroyed — is sealed by a passover kept to the LORD in Jerusalem, at the one sanctuary the law appointed and with the full ritual the law prescribed. Gill's chronology and Ellicott's exegesis meet in a single impression: that everything the reformation had accomplished converged upon this one keeping of the feast, in this one year, at this one place, exactly as the recovered book had commanded. The date is not an idle number but the historian's way of stamping the whole work with the mark of obedience.
24:
With public idolatry swept away, Josiah turns to the hidden and domestic superstitions, and the witnesses catalogue them. The reformed John Gill notes only that the workers with familiar spirits and the wizards were such as were never to be allowed among the Israelites. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sees the movement of the verse clearly: after abolishing public idolatry, the king attacked the various forms of private superstition — the necromancers, or workers with familiar spirits, the teraphim or household images, and the idols, which he renders with some scorn "the dunglings," a word Gesenius takes for idol-blocks and Ewald for doll-images. He fastens on the phrase "that were spied," calling it significant: many idols had doubtless been concealed by their worshippers, and had to be searched out; and "put away" he glosses as strictly consumed, under the law of Deuteronomy 18:9 and Leviticus 20:27.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the whole as the conclusion of the reign's great work: as Josiah kept the passover in perfect accord with the law, so he exterminated the necromancers, the teraphim, and all the abominations of idolatry throughout Judah and Jerusalem, in order to set up — that is, to carry out and bring into force — the words of the law written in the book that Hilkiah had found in the house of the Lord. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and John Wesley fill in the identifications. Clarke refers the familiar spirits to his note on the earlier apostasy, and takes "the images" for the teraphim of Genesis 31:19. Wesley reads the several words — images, idols, abominations — as heaped up on purpose to show that all the instruments and monuments of idolatry were destroyed, and lays weight, with Ellicott, on "spied": all that were discovered, not only those in the places of worship but those the priests or zealots had removed and endeavoured to hide.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rounds off the reformation with a fitting completeness. Having cleansed the temple, the city, the country and the northern shrines of open idolatry, and having gathered the people to the passover, Josiah now hunts out the private and occult remnants — the fortune-teller's art, the household god, the hidden idol — so that nothing might be left standing against the recovered law. The whole labour, Keil and Delitzsch insist, was governed by that one aim throughout: to establish in deed the words of the book that had been found.
25:
The historian sets his highest seal on Josiah, and the witnesses both echo the praise and feel its tension with what follows. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry calls him one of the best kings that ever sat on the throne of David — as Hezekiah was a nonesuch for faith, so Josiah was a nonesuch for sincerity and zeal in a work of reformation. Henry unfolds the fourfold commendation of the verse: that he turned to the Lord from whom his fathers had revolted; that he did it with his heart and soul, making heart-work of his religion; that he did it with all his heart and soul and might, with a vigour and courage without which he could never have broken through the difficulties before him; and that he did it according to all the law of Moses, his zeal never betraying him into irregularity but walking always by rule.

The other witnesses guard the verse against a seeming contradiction. The reformed John Gill observes that the very same thing is said of Hezekiah, and reconciles the two by allowing that each king excelled the other in different things. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the same line: a writer would not measure such words by strict proportion, and there is no need to force a formal contradiction with the like eulogy of Hezekiah; still, the narrative does suggest that the Mosaic law had not been so rigorously carried out by any earlier king. He adds, from Jeremiah 22:15, that Josiah's merit lay not only in strict worship but in his righteousness as a judge. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke ranks him with, and in one respect above, even his greatest forebear: perhaps no king from David's time, morally considered, sat on the throne so exemplary in his own conduct and so thoroughly zealous in God's work — David was a greater but not a better man than Josiah.

Yet the praise stands on the edge of a precipice, and the grammatical-historical witnesses will not let the reader forget it. Keil and Delitzsch hear already in this verse the approach of doom: with the very word that crowns Josiah, the historian introduces the account of the kingdom's destruction, for "notwithstanding" all this the Lord turned not from his wrath. Henry frames the coming paradox as a mystery of providence — that after such a reformation one would have expected nothing but prosperity, and yet both king and kingdom are soon under a cloud, so that we must say with the psalmist, in Psalms 36:6, that God's righteousness is as the great mountains but his judgments a great deep. The witnesses do not divide over the man; they agree that a better reformer never reigned, and that the sequel, far from tarnishing his praise, only deepens the wonder of God's dealings with his people.
26:
The praise of Josiah is immediately shadowed by a terrible "notwithstanding," and the witnesses labour to hold the king's goodness and the nation's doom together. The reformed John Gill locates the reason in the people's insincerity: for all the great reformation, and for all that Josiah did heartily as to the Lord, the people did not turn with their whole heart but feignedly, and so the fierceness of the Lord's wrath against Judah was not withdrawn. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley expands the same diagnosis: though the king was most hearty in his repentance and acceptable to God, so that the judgment was delayed for his lifetime, the people were in general corrupt and secretly averse from his reformation, as the complaints of Jeremiah and Zephaniah show, and as the event proved — for as soon as Josiah was gone, his children, his princes and his people greedily returned to their old abominations.

On why the sins of a past generation should fall on this one, the witnesses reason together. Wesley gives several grounds at once: the guilt of Manasseh and of the men of his generation, justly punished in their heirs who repeated their crimes; God's sovereign right to punish when he sees fit; his declared purpose to visit the iniquity of the fathers upon the children; and, above all, that these men had never sincerely repented of their own sins or their fathers'. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads "the great heat of his wrath, wherewith his wrath burned," and points to the provocations of Manasseh named in the predictions of Jeremiah 15:4 and Zephaniah. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states it most bleakly: it was of no use to try this fickle and radically depraved people any longer; they were merely respited during the life of Josiah.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the reformation was real in the king and hollow in the people, that a forced conformity is not repentance, and that God, who knows the heart, would not recall a sentence which an outward reform had not inwardly earned. The verse becomes for them the hinge of the whole history — the point at which it is settled that Josiah's zeal, glorious as it was, could postpone but not prevent the ruin, because the sin that had drawn down the wrath still lived, unrepented, in the hearts of those he governed.
27:
The sentence long threatened is now spoken in the plainest words, and the witnesses receive it as the irrevocable decree of God. The reformed John Gill weighs the phrase "out of my sight": it does not mean out of God's all-seeing eye, from which nothing can be hidden, but out of his special care and protection — Judah is to be suffered to be carried into captivity as Israel was. And Gill stresses the settledness of it: this the Lord had said in his heart, the decree was gone forth, and it was irrevocable.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse the same way, as the divine resolve announced to the people through the prophets, especially Jeremiah and Zephaniah. The Lord will put away Judah from his face just as he put away Israel, according to 2 Kings 17:20, and will cast off the city Jerusalem which he had chosen, and the house of which he had said, "My name shall be there." The word, they observe, expresses a decree — no longer a warning to be averted, but a purpose declared.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on the tender pathos of the clause "the house of which I said, My name shall be there." God's original promise concerning the temple, he explains, was made upon conditions expressed in sundry places, which the people broke; and so God justly made them to know his breach of promise — that is, held himself no longer bound by a pledge whose terms they had violated. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the rejection of Judah is now a fixed thing, spoken by God and published by his prophets; that the election of Jerusalem and its temple, so long the ground of the nation's confidence, cannot shelter a people who have broken the covenant on which it rested; and that even the deepest mercy of God has conditions, so that the very sanctuary he chose may be cast off when those who worship in it have cast off him. It is the darkest word in the chapter, and the witnesses let it stand in its full severity.
28:
The formal closing-notice of Josiah's reign turns the witnesses briefly back over all he had done and forward to the disaster about to strike. The reformed John Gill, glossing "the rest of the acts of Josiah, and all that he did," understands them of his great work in abolishing idolatry and restoring the true worship of God — the deeds this and the preceding chapters have recorded, the annals of which are referred, as with every king, to the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look through the formula to what it prefaces. The predicted catastrophe, they say, was now brought to pass by the expedition of Necho king of Egypt against Assyria; and they pause to identify the Pharaoh named in the next verse — Necho, the second of that name, the sixth king of the twenty-sixth Saite dynasty, son of Psammetichus and grandson of the first Necho, a ruler celebrated, as Herodotus and Diodorus report, for his enterprises. Thus the bare chronicler's sentence stands on the threshold between the reign just summed up and the sudden ruin that is to end it, and Keil treats the verse less as a conclusion than as the doorway through which the last act of the history enters.

The witnesses do not divide, and the very brevity of the notice carries a weight they both feel. The historian, having praised Josiah above all the kings and having recorded the unalterable sentence on Judah, now files the king's reign under the usual formula — are they not written in the book of the chronicles? — as if to say that the outward record of his acts belongs to the common annals, while Scripture hastens on to the one thing that most concerns it: how this best of kings met his death, and how his death opened the way for the desolation of his people. The reticence is itself a judgment of proportion; the reformer whose zeal has filled two chapters is dismissed, for his remaining deeds, in a single formulaic line, so that the narrative may turn without delay to Megiddo. Gill's backward glance at the reformation and Keil's forward glance at Necho meet in the same verse, so that the closing formula becomes a hinge between the glory of the reign and the tragedy of its end.
29:
The reign that had been all reformation ends abruptly on a battlefield, and the witnesses gather to explain the campaign and the king's fatal part in it. The reformed John Gill identifies Pharaoh-nechoh, called by the Targum "the lame," as the Necho whom Herodotus makes the son of Psammetichus, reigning in Egypt in Josiah's last days. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the expedition: toward the end of Josiah's reign Necho went up against the king of Asshur to the Euphrates, and they furnish a full account of the Pharaoh from Herodotus, Diodorus and the Egyptian records.

The identity of "the king of Assyria" whom Necho marched against sets the witnesses debating. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and John Wesley take him to be the king of Babylon — Nabopolassar, who had by now conquered the Assyrian and become head of that empire, so that he is still called the Assyrian — the quarrel being, Clarke thinks, over Carchemish on the Euphrates, which the Babylonian had seized. Charles Ellicott weighs the chronology more finely: if Nineveh had already fallen, then Nabopolassar of Babylon must be meant; but if, as Eusebius and Jerome represent, Nineveh did not fall till a year or two after Necho's march of about 609 B.C., then the expedition was really directed against a king of Assyria in the strict sense, one of the shadowy successors of Assurbanipal. Necho's later and greater defeat at Carchemish, he notes, lay still in the future.

On Josiah's motive and his death the witnesses again reason together. Ellicott supposes he went out against Necho as a vassal of Assyria, resenting the Egyptian's trespass on territory he counted his own; the battle at Megiddo in the plain of Jezreel shows that Necho had come by sea and landed at Accho, for otherwise Josiah need not have marched so far north. "When he had seen him," Ellicott takes to mean at the very outset of the encounter, the moment they came in sight; and he recalls from 2 Chronicles 35:22 that Josiah was wounded by the Egyptian archers and carried off in a dying state. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley leaves Josiah's purpose open — whether to defend his own country from Necho's inroads or to assist the king of Babylon, with whom he seems to have been in league — but reads "slew him at Megiddo" as the giving of the death-wound there, though the king did not die till he reached Jerusalem; and he ventures that Josiah, having no clear call to this war, may have received his wound as a rebuke for his rashness. The witnesses do not divide over the sorrow of it. They agree that the best of Judah's kings, drawn without a clear commission into a quarrel of empires, fell at Megiddo, and that his fall was the first stroke of the desolation the chapter has just foretold.
30:
The dying king is borne home, and a new king is raised in haste, and the witnesses attend to both the manner of Josiah's death and the choice of his successor. The reformed John Gill reads the plain words closely: the servants took the mortally wounded king from the chariot in which he was hurt and put him in another, where he died on the way, so that he is called dead though the wound, not the journey's end, had killed him. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes the same grammatical point — that "dead" should be taken as "dying," for Josiah was not yet dead but mortally wounded at Megiddo, carried in a dying state to Jerusalem, and there died and was buried, as 2 Chronicles 35:24 relates; he adds that the great mourning was at Hadadrimmon in the valley of Megiddo.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill out the scene from the Chronicler: the servants took the severely wounded king, by his own command, from his war-chariot into his second chariot and drove him to Jerusalem, where he died and was buried. The depth of the national grief they measure by Zechariah 12:11, where the lamentation for the Messiah is likened to the mourning of Hadad-rimmon in the valley of Megiddo, and by the account of the whole nation's lamentation in 2 Chronicles 35:25, which records that Jeremiah himself made a dirge for Josiah.

On the raising of Jehoahaz the witnesses note an irregularity. Charles Ellicott observes that "the people of the land" took and anointed him though he was not the eldest son — a fact plain from v.36 — perhaps thinking him a more capable prince amid the emergencies of the time, an estimate that Jeremiah 22:10 shows to have been mistaken. John Wesley agrees that Jehoahaz was younger than Jehoiakim yet preferred by the people, either because Jehoiakim declined the crown for fear of Pharaoh or because Jehoahaz was the more warlike prince, the young lion of Ezekiel 19:3. The witnesses do not divide. They gather around a single mournful transition: the king who had turned the whole nation to God is carried home a corpse amid a lamentation that became proverbial, and in his place the people set, by their own hand and against the ordinary succession, a younger son whose brief reign would only hasten the ruin.
31:
The last movement of the chapter begins with the brief and unhappy reign of Jehoahaz, and the witnesses fix his identity and his character. The reformed John Gill identifies him with the Shallum of Jeremiah's prophecy. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm it: Jeremiah significantly calls him Shallum, "to whom it is requited"; he reigned only three months and did evil in the eyes of the Lord as all his fathers had done. The people who had preferred him to his elder brother had evidently set great hopes on him, expecting his strength and energy to avert the danger from Necho; but Ezekiel 19:3 compares him to a young lion that learned to catch prey and devoured men, and was then taken in the nations' pit and led with nose-rings to Egypt — a tyrant, in the estimate of Ezekiel and Josephus alike, disposed to violence.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke settles the chronology of the brothers: Jehoahaz was not Josiah's eldest son, for he was twenty-three at his accession and reigned but three months, while his brother Eliakim, set up in his place, was then twenty-five; Eliakim was therefore the elder, and Jehoahaz was raised to the throne by the people as being of a more active and martial spirit. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the reign in the long shadow that now falls over Jerusalem: after Josiah was laid in his grave the city saw not a good day, but one trouble came after another until, within twenty-two years, it was destroyed. Jehoahaz, a younger son, was made king by the people of the land, probably because he was of a more warlike genius than his elder brother and likely to avenge his father's death — which the people, Henry suspects, cared for more, in point of honour, than the carrying-on of their father's reformation; and the issue answered to their choice.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the people's choice was governed by martial hope rather than piety, that the young king they crowned was at heart a violent man, and that his three months' reign — the very brevity of it — opens the rapid unravelling of all Josiah had built. The mother's name and city, Hamutal the daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah, the historian records in passing; but the weight of the verse, for these witnesses, lies in the ominous return of the old verdict, "he did that which was evil," upon the very first of Josiah's sons to reign.
32:
The old refrain of judgment, silent through Josiah's reign, returns at once upon his son, and the witnesses read the brief verdict against Jehoahaz. The reformed John Gill glosses "that which was evil in the sight of the Lord" with a single word — idolatry. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley identifies the pattern he followed: "his fathers" here means his grandparents, Manasseh and Amon, whose idolatry he restored, undoing what his father had destroyed; and Wesley repeats the mournful note that Jerusalem saw not a good day after Josiah was buried, but trouble upon trouble till within twenty-two years it was destroyed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears behind the verdict the lament of Ezekiel 19:1, where Jehoahaz is the young lion that "devoureth men" — an allusion, he says, to the king's oppressive rapacity and shameless abuse of power in so short a reign. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the sobering lesson of the phrase "according to all that his fathers had done": though Jehoahaz reigned too briefly to show much of his character, he had already chosen his patterns and shown whom he meant to follow, and so is reckoned to have done all the evil of those he proposed to imitate. It is of great consequence, Henry warns, whom young people choose to emulate — an error in that choice is fatal — and he points to the example-and-warning of Philippians 3:17.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the evil of Jehoahaz was the old calf-and-idol apostasy of his wicked forebears, deliberately resumed; that even a reign of three months was long enough to declare a man's allegiance; and that the son of the best of kings, given every advantage of a godly upbringing and example, chose nonetheless to walk in the way of his grandfather Manasseh rather than his father Josiah. The verdict that had been suspended while Josiah lived falls again the moment he is gone, and the witnesses hear in its swift return the confirmation of the sentence already passed upon Judah — that the reformation had changed the throne for a season but not the heart of the nation.
33:
The people's warlike hope is dashed within three months, and the witnesses trace the captivity of Jehoahaz and the tribute laid on the land. The reformed John Gill places Riblah in the land of Hamath in Syria, which Jerome identified with Antioch near the fountain of Daphne, and supposes Jehoahaz had gone thither with an army — to avenge his father's death on Necho, or to assist the king of Babylon, or both — where Pharaoh met, took and bound him. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reasons out the cause of the binding: Jehoahaz, otherwise Shallum, likely raised an army, met Necho on his return from Carchemish, was beaten, taken prisoner, put in chains, and carried to Egypt where he died.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects and enlarges the picture. Riblah, he says, lay in a strong position on the Orontes, commanding the caravan route from Palestine to the Euphrates; Necho had advanced there after Megiddo and taken up his quarters, as Nebuchadnezzar would afterwards. Josephus relates that the Pharaoh summoned Jehoahaz to his camp, and Ezekiel 19:4 — "he was taken in their pit" — suggests he got the young king into his power by fraud. Ellicott also lays to rest the old notion, drawn from Herodotus, that Necho had captured Jerusalem: the Kadytis Herodotus names is really Kadesh on the Orontes, not the holy city. On the tribute he prefers the smaller figures, noting that the Septuagint's hundred talents of gold is a copyist's error and that the true reading may be the Syriac ten. Keil and Delitzsch, weighing the same clause, discuss whether the Hebrew should read that Necho put him in bands "that he might not reign" or "when he had become king," preferring on the whole the sense that the deposition is meant.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sums up the reason for Necho's severity: he bound Jehoahaz either because the young man had presumed to take the kingdom without the Pharaoh's consent, or because he had renewed the war against him. The witnesses do not divide over the outcome. They agree that the warlike prince on whom the people had staked their hopes was seized at Riblah almost before he had reigned, deposed and fettered by the very Egyptian he was thought able to withstand, and that the land was at once burdened with a heavy fine of silver and gold — the first exaction of a foreign master over the kingdom that Josiah's death had left exposed. The young lion, as Ezekiel had it, was taken in the pit and led away, and Judah began to pay the price of its ruin.
34:
Necho now sets a king of his own choosing over Judah, and the witnesses dwell on the deposition of one brother, the elevation of another, and the changing of his name. The reformed John Gill notes that Eliakim was made king "in the room of Josiah his father," not in the room of Jehoahaz, because Necho would not allow Jehoahaz any lawful right to the throne; the Pharaoh deposed the younger and set up the elder brother. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw the same inference — that Necho did not acknowledge the reign of Jehoahaz, whom the people had installed without his consent — and read the change of name as a sign of dependence, after the ancient custom by which masters renamed their servants, as in Genesis 41:45 and Daniel 1:7.
