The witnesses find a curious significance in the name itself. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains that Eliakim means "God shall arise" and Jehoiakim "Jehovah shall arise," names of the same import, the change serving only to show Necho's supremacy and that Jehoiakim was his vassal — just as Mattaniah was later changed to Zedekiah, and Daniel and his companions given Babylonian names. Keil and Delitzsch notice a striking restraint in it: whereas such imposed names were usually borrowed from heathen deities, Necho gave, and Nebuchadnezzar later gave, genuine Israelite names — Jehoiakim, "Jehovah will set up," and Zedekiah, "righteousness of Jehovah" — so that the vassal kings were not treated exactly as ordinary subjects. Charles Ellicott, agreeing the change marked the new king as Necho's creature, adds the conjecture that the Pharaoh may have fancied a resemblance between the divine name Yahu and Aah, the Egyptian moon-god; and he supposes Eliakim, the elder son, may have paid court to Necho, or been chosen as likely to prove the stronger king.

On the fate of the deposed Jehoahaz the witnesses simply record the fulfilment of prophecy. "He came to Egypt, and died there," and Ellicott notes that Jeremiah had foretold it in Jeremiah 22:10. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley, briefly, reads the whole transaction as an exercise of dominion — the giving of names being reckoned an act of lordship, which parents did to their children and conquerors to their vassals. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Judah's kings were now made and unmade at an Egyptian's word, their very names changed to publish their servitude, and that the crown passed, for the first time in David's line, from one brother to another at the bidding of a foreign master.
35:
The tribute Necho imposed must now be wrung from the people, and the witnesses observe how Jehoiakim raised it. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that Jehoiakim gave to Pharaoh the hundred talents of silver and the talent of gold that had been laid as tribute on the land. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the machinery: Necho had placed Jehoiakim on the throne simply as his viceroy, to raise and collect his taxes; and though the avowed principle was that each man was assessed in proportion to his property, Clarke fears this bad king was not in fact governed by so fair a rule.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look ahead over the whole eleven years the verse begins to record, and paint the character of the reign. Jehoiakim reigned in the spirit of his ungodly forefathers; Jeremiah, in Jeremiah 22:13, represents him as a bad prince who enriched himself by the unjust oppression of his people, his eyes and heart set on nothing but gain, on innocent blood to shed it, and on oppression and violence to do them; and Josephus accordingly describes him as unjust and wicked by nature, neither reverent toward God nor equitable toward men. The exaction here, that is, was of a piece with the whole reign.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks the bitter reversal at the root of it: formerly the Israelites had spoiled the Egyptians at the Exodus, and now the Egyptians spoil Israel — see, he says, what woeful changes sin makes. The money is squeezed out of the people "of every one according to his taxation" and handed over to a foreign king, while the puppet on the throne uses the occasion, as Keil and Clarke agree, for his own oppression. Henry notes too that this poverty did the king no spiritual good, and that Jehoiakim, exacting the silver and gold from a burdened land, only prepared himself for the greater judgments to come.

The witnesses do not divide. The tribute of v.33 is not a single humiliation but the opening of a settled bondage, in which a king set up by Egypt grinds his own subjects to pay his master and to feed his own greed. Where Josiah had gathered the nation to a passover of joy, his son now gathers from it a levy of servitude; the golden year of the reformation has given way, within months, to a reign of extortion, and the very people who had kept the feast in the one place the law appointed are taxed head by head to enrich a heathen court. The witnesses let the contrast stand as the measure of how far, and how fast, Judah had fallen from its brief noon of obedience.
36:
The regnal formula for Jehoiakim gives the witnesses little to expound, and they draw out what the numbers and names imply. The reformed John Gill fixes the one point the figures settle: Jehoiakim was twenty-five years old at his accession, and therefore two years older than his brother Jehoahaz, who had been deposed — confirming that the people had passed over the elder to crown the younger and more warlike son. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the frame of the reign: Jehoiakim reigned eleven years in Jerusalem, in the spirit of his ungodly forefathers, and his mother was Zebudah the daughter of Pedaiah of Rumah, a town whose site they briefly consider.

The witnesses do not divide, and the bare notice takes its colour from what surrounds it. The eleven years here allotted to Jehoiakim stand in pointed contrast to the three months of his brother: the king Necho chose kept his throne, while the king the people chose was already dead in Egypt. Yet length of reign is no mark of favour, for these eleven years, as Jeremiah's witness and the following history show, were years of oppression and bloodshed under a prince who did evil in the sight of the Lord. The historian, having recorded the age, the length, and the mother's name and city after his settled manner, prepares the reader for the verdict that the next verse will pronounce.

What the witnesses hear in the formula is the quiet machinery of the kingdom's decline. Three kings' reigns are now compressed into the close of this single chapter — Josiah fallen at Megiddo, Jehoahaz carried to Egypt, and Jehoiakim set up as a foreign vassal — and the measured cadence of the regnal notice, with its ages and years and mothers' names, goes on unbroken even as the throne of David passes from a reformer to his sons and from independence into servitude. The very ordinariness of the formula, applied now to a puppet king in a doomed city, is part of the chapter's sombre close, and the witnesses let the plain record speak the tragedy it does not name.
37:
The chapter closes as so many reigns in this book have closed, with the flat verdict of condemnation, and the witnesses read it over the last king it names. The reformed John Gill refers "all that his fathers had done" to Amon and Manasseh, whose evil Jehoiakim reproduced. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply register the formula, having already, in the previous verse, filled in from Jeremiah the oppression and violence that gave it substance. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is more vehement: Jehoiakim was a most unprincipled and oppressive tyrant, whose character Jeremiah draws at length in Jeremiah 22:13 and following — and Jeremiah, Clarke notes, was in the land at the time and an eyewitness of the king's abominations.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the moral of the whole ending. The tribute that made Jehoiakim poor did not make him good: notwithstanding the rebukes of Providence he was under, by which he should have been humbled and reformed, he did that which was evil in the sight of the Lord, and so prepared against himself greater judgments — for such God will surely send if the lesser do not do their work. Henry lifts his eyes, at the last, above the wreck: a little time and slender abilities often suffice to undo the good that pious men have laboured years to effect, and yet, blessed be God, the good work which he begins by his regenerating Spirit cannot be done away, but withstands all changes and temptations.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Jehoiakim, like his brother before him, turned back to the idolatry of Manasseh and Amon, and that his was a reign of tyranny and innocent blood; and they close the chapter on the same note with which its darker half began — that after Josiah, Judah's kings did evil, hardened under correction, and heaped up the wrath that would soon overwhelm the city. So the great reformation ends in a verse of condemnation, and the historian, having shown the best of kings and then his sons, leaves the reader with the sentence already passed on Judah ringing over a throne that has learned nothing from its father's zeal.
